symbols.name: M
symbols.def: Number of clusters in the finite experiment.

symbols.name: m
symbols.def: Common number of units in each cluster.

symbols.name: N
symbols.def: Population size, N = M m.

symbols.name: C_1, ..., C_M
symbols.def: Partition of the finite population into M clusters of size m.

symbols.name: pbar
symbols.def: Target mean treatment saturation across clusters.

symbols.name: u
symbols.def: Generic cluster-level treatment saturation.

symbols.name: d
symbols.def: Centered saturation coordinate d = u - pbar.

symbols.name: delta_x
symbols.def: Dirac probability law at x.

symbols.name: nu
symbols.def: Continuous-relaxation law for first-stage cluster saturations.

symbols.name: pi
symbols.def: Finite design vector of realized cluster saturations.

symbols.name: hat_tau_DM
symbols.def: Difference-in-means estimator generated by the two-stage randomized saturation design.

symbols.name: tau
symbols.def: Average direct-effect contrast under the homogeneous linear-in-share working model at the target policy saturation pbar.

symbols.name: V_0
symbols.def: Constant term in the scoped Cai-style leading-criterion expansion attached to the chosen homogeneous linear-in-share isolated-cluster specialization.

symbols.name: V_1
symbols.def: Coefficient on the centered second moment m_2(nu) in the scoped Cai-style leading-criterion expansion attached to the chosen specialization.

symbols.name: V_3
symbols.def: Coefficient on the centered third moment m_3(nu) in the scoped Cai-style leading-criterion expansion attached to the chosen specialization.

symbols.name: V_4
symbols.def: Coefficient on the centered fourth-moment correction m_4(nu) - m_2(nu)^2 in the scoped Cai-style leading-criterion expansion attached to the chosen specialization.

symbols.name: s
symbols.def: Candidate centered second moment s = m_2(nu).

symbols.name: a, b, c
symbols.def: Dual/KKT multipliers for the profiled bounded-support moment program.

symbols.name: C_rd(nu)
symbols.def: Lipschitz constant controlling the change in V after perturbing the support masses by O(M^(-1)) and support points by O(m^(-1)).

symbols.name: Y_{ji}(z,u)
symbols.def: Potential outcome for unit i in cluster j under own treatment z and cluster saturation u.

symbols.name: T_pbar
symbols.def: Centering map T_pbar(u) = u - pbar.

symbols.name: D_pbar
symbols.def: Centered support domain D_pbar = [-pbar, 1 - pbar].

symbols.name: N(pbar)
symbols.def: Class of admissible saturation laws with mean pbar.

symbols.name: N_s(pbar,s)
symbols.def: Slice of admissible laws with centered second moment equal to s.

symbols.name: m_r(nu)
symbols.def: Centered r-th saturation moment of nu for r in {2,3,4}.

symbols.name: V(nu)
symbols.def: Quartic law-level leading-criterion functional obtained by plugging the centered saturation moments of nu into the scoped coefficient tuple (V_0, V_1, V_3, V_4).

symbols.name: F(s)
symbols.def: Profiled inner value obtained by minimizing the quartic integrand over N_s(pbar,s).

symbols.name: Q_{a,b,c}(d)
symbols.def: Quartic dual residual polynomial indexed by the multiplier tuple (a,b,c).

symbols.name: G_{a,c}(s)
symbols.def: Outer quadratic certificate function G_{a,c}(s) = V_0 + a + c s - V_4 s^2 indexed by (a,c).

symbols.name: Pi_{M,m}(pbar)
symbols.def: Class of exact finite saturation vectors on the 1/m grid with mean pbar.

symbols.name: L_M(pi)
symbols.def: Empirical saturation law induced by the finite vector pi.

symbols.name: pi_rd
symbols.def: Finite saturation vector produced by the grid-split rounding construction.

symbols.name: nu_w
symbols.def: Witness law nu_w = (2/3) delta_0 + (1/3) delta_1 used in the skewed strict-improvement example.

assumptions.condition: The finite population is partitioned into M clusters C_1, ..., C_M of common size m.
Potential outcomes for units in cluster C_j depend only on assignments within C_j and not on assignments in C_l for l != j.
Within each cluster, the working-model contribution of a realized assignment to the variance of hat_tau_DM depends on the assignment only through the cluster treatment share u.

novel.justification: This is realistic for two-stage saturation studies in which peer exposure is deliberately coarsened to saturation shares and cluster composition is approximately homogeneous, such as village or classroom interventions designed around anonymous within-cluster mixing.

assumptions.condition: In the note's specialized working model, for each treatment state z in {0,1} the within-cluster outcome surface Y_{ji}(z,u) is approximated by an affine function of the centered share d = u - pbar, with working-model coefficients that are common across clusters in the block-fixed isolated-cluster specialization.

novel.justification: This fixes only the outcome-side working approximation used for the bridge: a homogeneous linear-in-share approximation that is natural in isolated partial-interference saturation studies. It does not by itself assert a fixed law-level quartic criterion or global optimality consequences.

assumptions.condition: The finite-population sequence used to define the randomized-saturation continuous-relaxation criterion is in the Cai-Pouget-Abadie-Airoldi asymptotic variance-expansion domain: M -> infinity, m -> infinity, the linear-interference potential-outcome parameters are uniformly bounded, and the regularity hypotheses of Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi (2022, Theorem 9) hold in the homogeneous/block-fixed isolated-cluster specialization.

novel.justification: standard: Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi (2022), Theorem 9 and Corollary 12, randomized-saturation linear-interference variance expansion

definitions.name: Admissible saturation laws
definitions.construction: For pbar in [0,1], N(pbar) = {nu in P([0,1]) : integral_[0,1] u d nu(u) = pbar}.

definitions.name: Centered saturation moments
definitions.construction: For r in {2,3,4}, m_r(nu) = integral_[0,1] (u - pbar)^r d nu(u).

definitions.name: Profiled inner value
definitions.construction: F(s) = inf_{nu in N_s(pbar,s)} integral_[0,1] [V_1 (u - pbar)^2 + V_3 (u - pbar)^3 + V_4 (u - pbar)^4] d nu(u).

definitions.name: Outer quadratic certificate
definitions.construction: For the necessary-and-sufficient global certificate, the outer certificate is the profiled value H(s) = V_0 + F(s) - V_4 s^2. The affine expression G^{lin}_{a,c}(s)=V_0+a+c s-V_4s^2 is only a sufficient lower-certificate obtained from one slice dual line; it is not necessary for global optimality.

definitions.name: Implementable saturation vectors
definitions.construction: For pbar in [0,1], Pi_{M,m}(pbar) = {pi in {0, 1/m, 2/m, ..., 1}^M : M^(-1) sum_{j=1}^M pi_j = pbar}.

definitions.name: Empirical law of a finite design
Grid-split rounding handle
definitions.construction: Given a K-atomic law nu = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell}, first round the masses w_ell to a vector of cluster counts summing to M, then replace each support point u_ell by one or two adjacent points in {0,1/m,...,1} with mixing weights chosen to preserve the contribution of that atom to the mean constraint up to the final balancing adjustment that enforces M^(-1) sum_j pi_j = pbar.

definitions.name: Support-pattern contact system
definitions.construction: Let Theta={theta=(pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4):0<=pbar<=1}. For k in {1,2,3}, define E_k(theta) as the set of tuples (w_1,...,w_k,d_1,...,d_k) satisfying w_i>=0, sum_i w_i=1, -pbar<=d_1<=...<=d_k<=1-pbar, and sum_i w_i d_i=0. Define the branch objective R_k^0(theta,w,d)=V_1 sum_i w_i d_i^2+V_3 sum_i w_i d_i^3+V_4{sum_i w_i d_i^4-(sum_i w_i d_i^2)^2}; equivalently one may add the common constant V_0, which does not affect argmin comparisons. This node is only notation for the finite support/contact families and exact-input branch objectives used by thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorithm: S1 is the singleton centered support {0}, S2 is the two-point centered family {-b,a} with mean-zero weights, and S3 is the three-point endpoint-interior family {-pbar,r,1-pbar} or its equivalent fixed-second-moment KKT parameterization from lem:explicit-profile-value. It does not assert a parameter-uniform semialgebraic partition or a global branch-selection map over Theta.

statements.statement: Under ass:equal-cluster-size, ass:isolated-partial-interference, ass:anonymous-share-specialization, ass:homogeneous-linear-share-working-model, and ass:cai-leading-expansion-domain, fix the note's chosen homogeneous/block-fixed isolated-cluster specialization of the Cai expansion, and let (V_0, V_1, V_3, V_4) denote the resulting scoped coefficient tuple after the isolated-cluster simplification V_2 = 0. For every feasible saturation vector pi in Pi_{M,m}(pbar), the corresponding o(N^{-1})-free leading comparison is exactly V(L_M(pi)). Consequently, if a feasible sequence pi^(n) in Pi_{M_n,m_n}(pbar) has empirical laws converging weakly to nu, then nu belongs to N(pbar) and the corresponding leading comparisons converge to V(nu). Moreover, along every regime M,m -> infinity with N pbar in Z, the infimum of the feasible leading criterion over Pi_{M,m}(pbar) converges to min_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu), and this asymptotic infimum is approached by grid-split rounding of a support-at-most-three minimizer.
statements.justification: This node is the honest optimizer-level bridge: after one fixes a particular Cai specialization and names its coefficient tuple, each feasible finite design has leading value V evaluated at its empirical law, and the feasible asymptotic optimum is pinned down by the quartic law program plus the rounding theorem.
statements.gap: CaiPougetAbadieAiroldi2022 supplies the randomized-saturation variance expansion and the isolated-cluster simplification V_2=0. The present note still does not derive from Cai that all design problems share one universal coefficient tuple; it fixes one homogeneous/block-fixed isolated-cluster specialization and then proves that the feasible asymptotic optimum in that specialized regime is governed by the induced quartic program.
statements.consumer: A design reader can move from the abstract quartic optimizer back to actual feasible saturation vectors without assuming that every admissible law is exactly realizable at finite M and m.
statements.proof_tex:
Proof. Fix the chosen homogeneous/block-fixed isolated-cluster specialization and its scoped coefficient tuple (V_0,V_1,V_3,V_4). Let pi be any feasible saturation vector in Pi_{M,m}(pbar). Under ass:equal-cluster-size the clusters have common size m, so pi is the finite design object entering the randomized-saturation expansion. Under ass:isolated-partial-interference and ass:anonymous-share-specialization, the comparison is restricted to isolated clusters and to within-cluster assignment features summarized by the saturation share. Under ass:homogeneous-linear-share-working-model, the note further chooses a homogeneous linear-in-share working approximation for the within-cluster outcome surface. Under ass:cai-leading-expansion-domain, lem:cai-randomized-saturation-moment-expansion gives the o(N^{-1})-free leading term

V_0 + V_1 mu_2(pi) + V_3 mu_3(pi) + V_4{mu_4(pi)-mu_2(pi)^2},

with V_2=0 and mu_r(pi)=M^{-1} sum_{j=1}^M (pi_j-pbar)^r for r=2,3,4. By def:empirical-law, L_M(pi)=M^{-1} sum_j delta_{pi_j}, so m_r(L_M(pi))=mu_r(pi) for r=2,3,4 by def:centered-moments. Therefore the finite leading comparison attached to pi is exactly V(L_M(pi)) by def:variance-functional.

Now let pi^(n) in Pi_{M_n,m_n}(pbar) be a feasible sequence with L_{M_n}(pi^(n)) converging weakly to nu. Because every pi^(n) belongs to Pi_{M_n,m_n}(pbar), def:implementable-vector-class and def:empirical-law give integral_[0,1] u dL_{M_n}(pi^(n))(u)=pbar for all n. The identity function u |-> u is bounded and continuous on [0,1], so lem:bounded-continuous-integrals-weakly-continuous yields integral_[0,1] u dnu(u)=pbar. Hence nu belongs to N(pbar) by def:admissible-law-class. Applying lem:empirical-centered-moment-continuity to the same weakly convergent sequence gives m_r(L_{M_n}(pi^(n))) -> m_r(nu) for r=2,3,4, so the exact finite leading values V(L_{M_n}(pi^(n))) converge to V(nu).

It remains to identify the asymptotic feasible optimum. For the lower bound, every feasible pi in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) has empirical law L_M(pi) in N(pbar) by the previous paragraph, and its leading value is V(L_M(pi)). Therefore

inf_{pi in Pi_{M,m}(pbar)} V(L_M(pi)) >= min_{eta in N(pbar)} V(eta)

for every feasible design size. For the matching upper bound, first suppose pbar is 0 or 1. Then lem:boundary-admissible-law-singleton gives N(pbar)={delta_pbar}, so min_{eta in N(pbar)}V(eta)=V(delta_pbar). Let pi^bd be the constant vector with every coordinate equal to pbar. Because pbar is itself a grid point in {0,1/m,...,1} and the average of pi^bd is pbar, we have pi^bd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) and L_M(pi^bd)=delta_pbar. Hence

inf_{pi in Pi_{M,m}(pbar)} V(L_M(pi)) <= V(L_M(pi^bd)) = V(delta_pbar)=min_{eta in N(pbar)}V(eta)

for every M and m, which together with the lower bound gives exact equality in the boundary branch. Now suppose 0<pbar<1. Then lem:compact-moment-program-attains gives a minimizer nu_0 of V over N(pbar), and prop:support-at-most-three gives a minimizer nu_star with V(nu_star)=V(nu_0)=min_{eta in N(pbar)}V(eta) and card(supp(nu_star))<=3. Along any regime M,m -> infinity with N pbar in Z, thm:rounding-loss produces pi_rd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) such that

V(L_M(pi_rd)) <= V(nu_star) + C_rd(nu_star)(M^{-1}+m^{-1}).

Taking limsup along the regime gives

limsup inf_{pi in Pi_{M,m}(pbar)} V(L_M(pi)) <= V(nu_star)=min_{eta in N(pbar)}V(eta),

while the lower bound gives the matching liminf inequality. Hence the feasible asymptotic infimum converges to min_{eta in N(pbar)}V(eta), and the grid-split rounded sequence approaches that value. This bridge remains scoped to the induced leading criterion after dropping the o(N^{-1}) remainder; it does not identify the exact non-leading finite-population variance with V.

statements.statement: For nu_star in N(pbar), let s_star = m_2(nu_star), and write H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4 s^2 for the profiled outer value from def:outer-certificate. Then nu_star belongs to argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu) if and only if there exist scalars a_star, b_star, c_star such that Q_{a_star,b_star,c_star}(d) >= 0 for all d in D_pbar, Q_{a_star,b_star,c_star}(d) = 0 for every d in T_pbar(supp(nu_star)), and H(s) >= V(nu_star) for all s in [0, pbar(1-pbar)] with equality at s = s_star.
statements.justification: The kernel must be a true global certificate for the nonconvex moment program, not a local first-order condition that drops the outer m_2^2 term.
statements.gap: CaiPougetAbadieAiroldi2022 optimizes within structured design families, while the archived proposal failed because its KKT condition ignored the nonlinear -V_4 m_2(nu)^2 residual; no published saturation-design paper gives a necessary-and-sufficient certificate over all bounded-support laws with fixed mean.
statements.consumer: A referee or practitioner can verify optimality of any candidate saturation law by checking one quartic residual on the interval and one scalar outer inequality.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Put f(d)=V_1 d^2+V_3 d^3+V_4 d^4 and let mu_star=(T_pbar)_#nu_star. By ass:budget-interior and lem:centered-pushforward-program, mu_star is a probability law on D_pbar with integral d dmu_star=0, and s_star=m_2(nu_star)=integral d^2 dmu_star. Also 0<=s_star<=pbar(1-pbar). By lem:profile-value-reduction, on each nonempty slice N_s(pbar,s) the value H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4 s^2 is exactly inf{V(nu):nu in N_s(pbar,s)}. First assume nu_star minimizes V over N(pbar). Then no law in its own slice can have smaller integral f, because V differs from integral f on that slice by the constant V_0 - V_4 s_star^2. Therefore nu_star is a fixed-s_star slice minimizer. By lem:bounded-moment-slice-duality there are a_star,b_star,c_star such that Q_{a_star,b_star,c_star}(d)>=0 for all d in D_pbar and Q_{a_star,b_star,c_star}(d)=0 on T_pbar(supp(nu_star)). Moreover, since nu_star is a global minimizer, every law in every slice has V at least V(nu_star). Taking the infimum over each slice and using lem:second-moment-slices-nonempty gives H(s)>=V(nu_star) for every s in [0,pbar(1-pbar)]. At s=s_star, nu_star itself belongs to the slice and is slice-minimizing, so H(s_star)=V(nu_star). Conversely, suppose the displayed quartic residual and outer-profile conditions hold. Let nu be arbitrary in N(pbar), and put s=m_2(nu). By lem:profile-value-reduction, V(nu)>=H(s). The assumed outer inequality gives H(s)>=V(nu_star). Hence V(nu)>=V(nu_star) for every admissible nu, so nu_star belongs to argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu). This proves both necessity and sufficiency.

statements.statement: For the quartic saturation objective V over the admissible law class N(pbar), there exists nu_star in argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu) such that card(supp(nu_star)) <= 3; when pbar in {0,1}, this optimizer is the unique admissible law delta_pbar.
statements.justification: A finite-atomic optimizer reduction is what converts the infinite-dimensional law problem into a tractable exact-design theorem.
statements.gap: Winkler1988 and Pinelis2012 provide extreme-point geometry for linear moment slices, but the published saturation-design literature does not combine that slice-wise result with the profiled nonlinear outer problem to deduce support-at-most-three for the actual quartic objective.
statements.consumer: This reduction feeds both the constructive skewed optimizer corollary and the finite-M implementation theorem.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. If pbar=0 or pbar=1, the boundary case is exactly lem:boundary-admissible-law-singleton: it gives N(pbar)={delta_pbar}, so the unique admissible law has support size one. Assume now 0<pbar<1. In this main branch, existence of a global minimizer is supplied by lem:compact-moment-program-attains; take one minimizer nu_0 and set s_0=m_2(nu_0). Let mu_0=(T_pbar)_#nu_0. By lem:centered-pushforward-program, mu_0 is supported on D_pbar, has mean zero, and has second moment s_0. On the slice N_s(pbar,s_0), lem:profile-value-reduction shows that minimizing V is exactly the same as minimizing the continuous affine functional mu |-> integral (V_1 d^2+V_3 d^3+V_4 d^4) dmu, because V_0-V_4 s_0^2 is constant on that slice. By lem:compact-convex-moment-slice, the corresponding centered moment slice is nonempty, compact, and convex, and the affine objective attains its minimum there. The support reduction in the same 0<pbar<1 branch is exactly lem:extreme-moment-slice-support: the minimizing face has an extreme-point minimizer mu_star on the same slice, and every such extreme point is supported on at most three points. Push mu_star forward by d |-> pbar+d and call the resulting law nu_star. By lem:centered-pushforward-program, nu_star belongs to N(pbar), has the same second moment s_0, and attains the same slice infimum as nu_0. Since nu_0 was globally minimizing and the slice value at s_0 equals the global value, nu_star is also globally minimizing. Its support has cardinality at most that of mu_star, hence at most three.

statements.statement: For all real V_1, V_3, V_4 and every 0 < pbar < 1, delta_pbar belongs to argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu) if and only if min_{d in D_pbar} (V_1 + V_3 d + V_4 d^2) >= 0.
statements.justification: The main corollary should say exactly when the stratified benchmark survives interference in the only branch where the symmetric candidate is algebraically defensible.
statements.gap: CaiPougetAbadieAiroldi2022 shows endpoint optimality can fail under interference but does not derive this global iff threshold, and the archived versions overclaimed threshold statements outside the V_1 > 0, V_4 > 0 branch.
statements.consumer: A designer who wants to keep within-cluster stratification gets a closed inequality deciding whether that benchmark is globally safe.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Let q(d)=V_1+V_3 d+V_4 d^2. The law delta_pbar has m_2=m_3=m_4=0, hence V(delta_pbar)=V_0. Assume first that q(d)>=0 for every d in D_pbar. Let nu in N(pbar), let mu=(T_pbar)_#nu, set s=integral d^2 dmu, m_3=integral d^3 dmu, and m_4=integral d^4 dmu. If V_4<=0, then V(nu)-V_0=integral d^2 q(d)dmu - V_4 s^2>=0. If V_4>0 and s=0, all centered moments vanish and again V(nu)=V_0. If V_4>0 and s>0, lem:pearson-centered-moment-bound gives t=m_3/s in D_pbar and m_4-s^2>=m_3^2/s. Hence V(nu)-V_0=V_1 s+V_3 m_3+V_4(m_4-s^2)>=s[V_1+V_3 t+V_4 t^2]=s q(t)>=0. Thus no admissible law improves on delta_pbar. Conversely, suppose q(d_0)<0 for some d_0 in D_pbar. Since q is continuous and 0<pbar<1, choose t in the interior (-pbar,1-pbar) with q(t)<0. Choose a,b>0 with a-b=t, a<=1-pbar, and b<=pbar. For example, take b small when t>=0 and a small when t<0. Let mu put mass b/(a+b) at a and mass a/(a+b) at -b, and let nu be the pushforward under d -> pbar+d. By lem:two-point-centered-objective, nu belongs to N(pbar) and V(nu)-V_0=ab q(t)<0. Hence V(nu)<V(delta_pbar), so delta_pbar is not globally optimal. The two implications prove the iff threshold.

statements.statement: At pbar = 1/3, V_1 = 1, V_3 = -10, and V_4 = 1, the law nu_w = (2/3) delta_0 + (1/3) delta_1 satisfies m_2(nu_w) = 2/9, m_3(nu_w) = 2/27, m_4(nu_w) - m_2(nu_w)^2 = 2/81, and V(nu_w) - V(delta_(1/3)) = -40/81 < 0.
statements.justification: A concrete strict-improvement witness prevents the positive-penalty threshold from reading like a purely symbolic branch condition.
statements.gap: The existing saturation-design papers do not display a finite feasible skewed law that beats the stratified benchmark by an explicit algebraic amount in this specialized quartic objective.
statements.consumer: The witness gives an immediately deployable counterexample showing why symmetric or endpoint heuristics can fail sharply.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. At pbar=1/3 the centered support points of nu_w=(2/3)delta_0+(1/3)delta_1 are -1/3 with mass 2/3 and 2/3 with mass 1/3. Therefore m_2(nu_w)=(2/3)(1/9)+(1/3)(4/9)=2/9, m_3(nu_w)=(2/3)(-1/27)+(1/3)(8/27)=2/27, and m_4(nu_w)=(2/3)(1/81)+(1/3)(16/81)=2/27. Thus m_4(nu_w)-m_2(nu_w)^2=2/27-4/81=2/81. Since delta_(1/3) has all centered moments equal to zero, for V_1=1,V_3=-10,V_4=1 we get V(nu_w)-V(delta_(1/3))=2/9-10(2/27)+2/81=(18-60+2)/81=-40/81<0.

statements.statement: Let nu = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} be in N(pbar). If M w_ell is an integer for every ell and m u_ell is an integer for every ell, then there exists pi in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) such that L_M(pi) = nu. In particular, these divisibility conditions already imply N pbar in Z. For the Bernoulli vertex (1 - pbar) delta_0 + pbar delta_1, exact attainability reduces to M pbar in Z. For the within-cluster stratified law delta_pbar, exact attainability reduces to m pbar in Z.
statements.justification: A continuous optimizer is only useful for experiment design if the law can be translated into an exact finite two-stage randomization when denominators line up.
statements.gap: CaiPougetAbadieAiroldi2022 passes to asymptotics and BasseFeller2016 studies finite two-stage analysis, but neither paper states the exact denominator-compatible realization theorem with separate cluster-mass and within-cluster-grid clauses.
statements.consumer: This theorem tells an experimentalist when the continuous optimum can be run exactly rather than merely approximated.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Fix a finite atomic admissible law nu = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} in N(pbar). By def:admissible-law-class, the weights are nonnegative, sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell = 1, each u_ell lies in [0,1], and sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell u_ell = pbar. Assume M w_ell is an integer for every ell and m u_ell is an integer for every ell. Put n_ell = M w_ell. Then n_ell is a nonnegative integer and sum_{ell=1}^K n_ell = M sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell = M. Form a saturation vector pi in [0,1]^M by listing the value u_ell exactly n_ell times, for ell = 1,...,K. Since m u_ell is an integer and 0 <= u_ell <= 1, every coordinate pi_j lies in the grid {0,1/m,2/m,...,1}. Its average saturation is M^{-1} sum_{j=1}^M pi_j = M^{-1} sum_{ell=1}^K n_ell u_ell = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell u_ell = pbar. Hence pi belongs to Pi_{M,m}(pbar) by def:implementable-vector-class. By def:empirical-law, L_M(pi) = M^{-1} sum_{j=1}^M delta_{pi_j} = sum_{ell=1}^K (n_ell/M) delta_{u_ell} = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} = nu. This proves exact finite attainability. The same divisibility conditions imply N pbar is an integer: using N = M m from the equal-cluster-size setup, N pbar = M m sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell u_ell = sum_{ell=1}^K (M w_ell)(m u_ell), an integer sum of integer products. For the Bernoulli vertex nu_B = (1-pbar) delta_0 + pbar delta_1, the support-grid conditions m*0 in Z and m*1 in Z are automatic. The mass-count conditions are M(1-pbar) in Z and M pbar in Z; because M is an integer, these are equivalent to M pbar in Z. Conversely, if an empirical law equals nu_B, exactly M pbar of the M coordinates must be equal to 1, so M pbar must be an integer. Thus exact attainability of the Bernoulli vertex reduces precisely to M pbar in Z. For the within-cluster stratified law delta_pbar, the mass condition M*1 in Z is automatic, and the support-grid condition is exactly m pbar in Z. Conversely, if L_M(pi) = delta_pbar, then every coordinate pi_j must equal pbar; since pi in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) has grid-valued coordinates, m pbar must be an integer. Thus exact attainability of delta_pbar reduces precisely to m pbar in Z.

statements.statement: For every nu_star in N(pbar) with card(supp(nu_star)) <= 3 and N pbar in Z, the grid-split rounding handle produces pi_rd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) such that V(L_M(pi_rd)) <= V(nu_star) + C_rd(nu_star) (M^(-1) + m^(-1)).
statements.justification: The finite-design theorem should not stop at exact compatibility because most empirical budgets land off the exact denominator grid.
statements.gap: The randomized-saturation literature discusses finite designs and large-cluster approximations, but it does not provide an optimizer-preserving O(M^(-1)+m^(-1)) loss bound for rounding a continuous quartic optimum.
statements.consumer: This gives a rate-certified recipe for implementing near-optimal skewed saturation laws in real experiments with finite M and m.
statements.proof_tex: Let nu_star in N(pbar) have support cardinality K <= 3, and write nu_star = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} with w_ell > 0 and u_ell in [0,1]. Let N = M m and assume N pbar is an integer. Apply lem:finite-atomic-grid-split-rounding with nu = nu_star. It gives a vector pi_rd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar), produced by the grid-split rounding construction, and a coupling gamma_rd of nu_star with L_M(pi_rd) whose average displacement is at most 6/M + 2/m. Apply lem:quartic-variance-coupling-lipschitz to this coupling. With L_V = 2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|, we obtain |V(L_M(pi_rd)) - V(nu_star)| <= L_V (6/M + 2/m). Hence V(L_M(pi_rd)) <= V(nu_star) + 6 L_V (M^{-1} + m^{-1}). Taking the rounding constant C_rd(nu_star) to be any admissible constant at least 6 L_V, for instance C_rd(nu_star)=6(2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|), gives the stated inequality. The proof uses only the support-size premise on nu_star; prop:support-at-most-three supplies that premise when nu_star is chosen as a continuous optimizer.

statements.statement: If V_3 = 0 and V_4 = 0, then V(nu) = V_0 + V_1 m_2(nu) for every nu in N(pbar). Hence V_1 > 0 implies delta_pbar minimizes V over nu in N(pbar), V_1 < 0 implies (1 - pbar) delta_0 + pbar delta_1 minimizes V over nu in N(pbar), and V_1 = 0 makes every nu in N(pbar) tie in V, hence all extremal-m_2 laws tie accordingly. The exact finite realization of the Bernoulli vertex uses M pbar in Z, whereas the exact finite realization of delta_pbar uses m pbar in Z.
statements.justification: The new interference theorem should collapse to the known SUTVA endpoint rule with the correct finite compatibility clauses.
statements.gap: CaiPougetAbadieAiroldi2022 proves the SUTVA endpoint comparison as a standalone result, but the present contribution is the noncircular reduction from the same quartic certificate together with the corrected integrality split.
statements.consumer: This sanity reduction is the benchmark a referee will use to verify that the interference theorem reproduces the established no-interference corner.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. If V_3=V_4=0, def:variance-functional gives V(nu)=V_0+V_1 m_2(nu) for every nu in N(pbar). By lem:centered-second-moment-extrema, 0<=m_2(nu)<=pbar(1-pbar). The lower endpoint is attained by delta_pbar and, when the budget is interior, only by delta_pbar. The upper endpoint is attained by the Bernoulli vertex (1-pbar)delta_0+pbar delta_1 and, for 0<pbar<1, only by that vertex. Therefore V_1>0 makes the lower endpoint delta_pbar optimal, V_1<0 makes the upper endpoint Bernoulli vertex optimal, and V_1=0 makes V identically equal to V_0 on N(pbar), so all admissible laws tie and in particular the extremal-m_2 laws tie. The exact finite-realization clauses are exactly the two specializations of thm:finite-attainability: the Bernoulli vertex requires and is required by M pbar in Z, whereas the within-cluster stratified law delta_pbar requires and is required by m pbar in Z.

statements.statement: Certified delivered content plus the remaining atlas-compression question. For every exact algebraic or symbolic parameter point theta=(pbar, V_1, V_3, V_4) with 0 <= pbar <= 1, thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorithm gives a terminating exact optimizer/certificate for the quartic program; lem:explicit-profile-value gives a closed-form profiled outer value H(s); and prop:explicit-two-point-cell gives the complete closed-form optimizer over the singleton/two-point class. The remaining open question is whether the exact elimination/CAD output used inside that constructive theorem can be compressed into a parameter-uniform human-readable symbolic atlas, especially for globally active three-point cells.
statements.justification: The honest residual is atlas compression. The discharged DAG already gives the exact-input optimizer/certificate, the closed-form profile H, and the complete singleton/two-point optimizer, but it does not separately prove a parameter-uniform atlas theorem over Theta.
statements.gap: No published paper maps the full coefficient space of the quartic saturation objective into compact human-readable three-point optimizer regions, and the present discharged core likewise stops short of a standalone uniform branch-atlas theorem.
statements.consumer: A reader gets an exact optimizer/certificate at each exact input and knows that the unresolved part is only the compression of the internal elimination output into a short symbolic atlas.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Fix an exact algebraic or symbolic parameter point theta=(pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4) with 0<=pbar<=1. The delivered constructive content follows from the discharged dependency spine. The exact-input optimizer/certificate is thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorithm: it terminates, returns a law nu_alg in N(pbar) minimizing V, and certifies optimality by exact candidate comparison together with the residual/profile checks from thm:global-certificate. The profiled outer value used in that certificate is explicit by lem:explicit-profile-value, which gives F(0)=0 and, for s in [0,pq], the closed-form slice formula F(s)=min{min_{t in I_2(s)} Phi_2(s,t), min_{r in I_3(s)} Phi_3(s,r)} and hence H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4s^2. The singleton/two-point subclass is completely solved by prop:explicit-two-point-cell, which reduces that subclass to the two cubic endpoint branches and their finite stationary-point candidate list. What is not delivered as a separate proved node is a parameter-uniform semialgebraic partition of Theta packaged as a standalone optimizer atlas. The constructive theorem uses elimination/CAD/Thom machinery internally at exact input, but the discharged DAG stops at that exact-input solver/certificate plus the closed-form slice formulas and two-point reduction. Therefore the remaining question is precisely atlas compression: whether one can rewrite the exact internal three-point elimination output as compact human-readable parameter regions and closed-form formulas. That is the only residual issue recorded here.

statements.statement: Under ass:equal-cluster-size, ass:isolated-partial-interference, ass:anonymous-share-specialization, ass:homogeneous-linear-share-working-model, and ass:cai-leading-expansion-domain, and only within the scoped bridge transcription of lem:variance-moment-reduction, along every regime M,m -> infinity with N pbar in Z the sharp feasible asymptotic o(N^{-1})-free leading surrogate for the randomized-saturation design variance equals min_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu). More precisely, if pi^(n) in Pi_{M_n,m_n}(pbar) is any feasible sequence along such a regime and L_{M_n}(pi^(n)) converges weakly to nu, then its asymptotic leading surrogate value is V(nu) >= min_{eta in N(pbar)} V(eta); and every minimizer nu_star with card(supp(nu_star)) <= 3 is asymptotically implementable and leading-surrogate-optimal along every such regime via thm:rounding-loss. Consequently, for 0 < pbar < 1 the interior optimal laws for this surrogate are characterized by thm:global-certificate; the stratified-law benchmark delta_pbar is asymptotically leading-surrogate-optimal if and only if min_{d in D_pbar} (V_1 + V_3 d + V_4 d^2) >= 0 by prop:positive-penalty-threshold; if pbar in {0,1}, then N(pbar) = {delta_pbar}, so the boundary benchmark is uniquely optimal for the same surrogate. The SUTVA degeneration is given by prop:sutva-corner. This is only the payoff for that one fixed homogeneous-share Cai leading surrogate; the exact non-leading finite-population variance problem and broader randomized-saturation design problems remain separate scope items.
statements.justification: This is the honest design-level import of the quartic program: for one fixed homogeneous-share Cai transcription and along the stated asymptotic regime, it identifies only the sharp leading surrogate value and the implementable optimizer route.
statements.gap: The standalone proved contribution is still the quartic V-program theory. The design-level payoff is only the two-sided bridge back to feasible randomized-saturation designs after one homogeneous isolated-share transcription has been fixed and only along regimes M,m -> infinity with N pbar in Z; it does not solve the exact finite-population randomized-saturation variance problem beyond that specialization and surrogate.
statements.consumer: A design reader learns exactly which asymptotic regime and which leading surrogate are certified, how to recognize the interior law-level optimizer, and how to implement a support-at-most-three minimizer up to o(1) rounding loss along that regime, while seeing that the exact finite-population variance problem remains outside this node.
statements.proof_tex:
Proof. Under ass:equal-cluster-size, ass:isolated-partial-interference, ass:anonymous-share-specialization, ass:homogeneous-linear-share-working-model, and ass:cai-leading-expansion-domain, lem:variance-moment-reduction proves the first sentence as a statement about the o(N^{-1})-free leading surrogate, not about the exact finite-population variance, along every regime M,m -> infinity with N pbar in Z. Fix such a regime. If pi^(n) in Pi_{M_n,m_n}(pbar) is any feasible sequence whose empirical laws converge weakly to nu, the same lemma gives nu in N(pbar) and convergence of the leading surrogate values to V(nu); the lower-bound part of that lemma then yields V(nu) >= min_{eta in N(pbar)}V(eta). Conversely, prop:support-at-most-three supplies a minimizer nu_star in argmin_{nu in N(pbar)}V(nu) with card(supp(nu_star)) <= 3, and the rounding part of lem:variance-moment-reduction, using thm:rounding-loss, yields along the chosen regime a feasible rounded sequence whose leading surrogate values converge to V(nu_star)=min_{eta in N(pbar)}V(eta).

When 0<pbar<1, the interior minimizers of the quartic program governing this surrogate are characterized exactly by thm:global-certificate. In the same interior branch, prop:positive-penalty-threshold gives

delta_pbar in argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu) if and only if min_{d in D_pbar} (V_1+V_3 d+V_4 d^2) >= 0.

Combining this law-level criterion with the scoped feasible asymptotic bridge shows that the stratified-law benchmark delta_pbar is asymptotically leading-surrogate-optimal exactly on that coefficient region. If pbar=0 or pbar=1, lem:boundary-admissible-law-singleton gives N(pbar)={delta_pbar}, so the boundary benchmark is uniquely optimal for the same surrogate. Finally, when V_3=V_4=0, prop:sutva-corner gives the SUTVA degeneration of the same quartic program, including the Bernoulli-vertex, tie, and finite-compatibility clauses. This is exactly the note's scoped design payoff for the chosen homogeneous-share Cai surrogate. The exact non-leading finite-population variance remains outside this proposition.

statements.statement: For 0 < pbar < 1, write p=pbar, q=1-p, C=pq, and A=q-p. For s in [0,C], let I_2(s)=[s/p-p, q-s/q] and I_3(s)=[-s/q, s/p]. Define Phi_2(s,t)=V_1 s+V_4 s^2+s(V_3 t+V_4 t^2) and Phi_3(s,r)=V_1 s+V_3(A s+(s-C)r)+V_4((1-3C)s+(s-C)(r^2+A r)). Then the fixed-second-moment profile satisfies F(0)=0 and, for all s in [0,C], F(s)=min{min_{t in I_2(s)} Phi_2(s,t), min_{r in I_3(s)} Phi_3(s,r)}; consequently H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4s^2. The first minimum is attained by the two-point centered law whose support points are the roots of x^2-tx-s=0. The second is attained by the endpoint-interior law on {-p,r,q} with weights (s+rq)/(p+r), (C-s)/((p+r)(q-r)), and (s-pr)/(q-r), with the endpoint limiting interpretation. Since the two displayed minimizations are quadratic over closed intervals, F and H are explicit piecewise-algebraic functions of (s,pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4).
statements.justification: The profiled certificate becomes a computable closed formula once the fixed-second-moment slice is reduced to the two exposed moment-boundary families.
statements.gap: The prior version left F(s) implicit; moment-set references give support bounds, but not this saturation-specific profile formula.
statements.consumer: This removes the hidden outer-profile dependency from thm:global-certificate and makes H(s) directly evaluable.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Work in centered coordinates X=u-p with p=pbar, q=1-p, C=pq, A=q-p, and D=[-p,q], using lem:centered-pushforward-program. At s=0, every centered law in the slice has X=0 almost surely, so F(0)=0; the endpoint intervals are I_2(0)=[-p,q] and I_3(0)=[0,0], and the displayed formulas give Phi_2(0,t)=0 on I_2(0) and Phi_3(0,0)=0. At s=C, E[(X+p)(q-X)]=C-s=0 and the nonnegative factor (X+p)(q-X) forces support on {-p,q}; hence the only feasible centered law is the two-point Bernoulli on {-p,q} with weights q and p. Here I_2(C)=[A,A], so the two-point branch is the endpoint law with t=A. The other endpoint interval is I_3(C)=[-p,q], which is not collapsed; it is the degenerate endpoint case of the {-p,r,q} branch because the middle weight has factor C-s=0. Correspondingly Phi_3(C,r)=V_1 C+V_3 A C+V_4(1-3C)C is constant for every r in I_3(C). Using A^2+C=1-3C, this common value equals Phi_2(C,A), so both displayed branches give the same endpoint law value at s=C. Now let 0<s<C. For any law in the slice, put m3=E X^3 and m4=E X^4. The lemma lem:fixed-second-moment-envelope gives t=m3/s in I_2(s), r=(m3-A s)/(s-C) in I_3(s), the lower boundary m4>=s^2+s t^2, attained by the two-root law, and the upper boundary m4<=(1-3C)s+(s-C)(r^2+A r), attained by the endpoint-interior law. If V_4>=0, then for the law under consideration V_1 s+V_3 m3+V_4 m4 is at least Phi_2(s,t), and the two-root law attains Phi_2(s,t) for every t in I_2(s). Hence the slice infimum is min_{t in I_2(s)} Phi_2(s,t). In this case every endpoint-interior boundary point has the same m3 as some two-root law and weakly larger m4, so its value is no smaller; thus the minimum over the union of the two displayed branches is the same number. If V_4<0, the same argument with the upper envelope gives the slice infimum as min_{r in I_3(s)} Phi_3(s,r), and every two-root boundary point is dominated by the endpoint-interior law with the same m3 and weakly larger m4; again this equals the minimum over the union of the two branches. Substituting m3=s t and m4=s^2+s t^2 gives Phi_2, and substituting m3=A s+(s-C)r and the upper-envelope value for m4 gives Phi_3. Therefore F(s)=min{min_{t in I_2(s)} Phi_2(s,t), min_{r in I_3(s)} Phi_3(s,r)}. Finally, def:outer-certificate gives H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4 s^2. Each displayed minimization is a quadratic polynomial over a closed interval with endpoints rational in s and p, so the value is obtained by checking endpoints and the algebraic stationary point when it lies in the interval. Hence F and H are explicit piecewise-algebraic functions of the stated parameters.

statements.statement: There is a complete, exact, terminating constructive optimizer for the quartic saturation program at exact input. The input is theta=(pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4) with 0<=pbar<=1 together with an exact real-closed-field representation of its coordinates, for example rational, algebraic, or other exact real-closed-field data, and the output is a law nu_alg in N(pbar) minimizing V. If pbar is 0 or 1, output delta_pbar; this boundary case is certified directly because N(pbar) is then the singleton {delta_pbar}. If 0<pbar<1, write p=pbar, q=1-p, C=pq, and A=q-p, then enumerate the finite support/contact subsystems: S1, the singleton centered support {0}; S2, the two-point centered supports {-b,a}, equivalently the endpoint cubic branches g_L(t)=p(p+t)(V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2) on [-p,A] and g_R(t)=q(q-t)(V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2) on [A,q] together with their endpoint and stationary equations; and S3, the three-point endpoint-interior centered supports {-p,r,q}, equivalently the KKT systems for the explicit profile branch Phi_3 over 0<=s<=C and -s/q<=r<=s/p, including every active boundary subset. Solve these finitely many polynomial systems by exact real-algebraic elimination, cylindrical algebraic decomposition, Thom encodings, and root isolation; discard infeasible roots; construct the associated laws and exact algebraic V-values; and choose a minimum. The chosen law is certified by exact candidate comparison and, on interior contact patterns, by constructing the quartic residual Q, checking Q>=0 on D_pbar by Sturm/root isolation, and checking the outer inequality H(s)>=V(nu_alg) on [0,C] using the explicit closed-form H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4s^2. Exact algebraic CAD/Thom computation at exact input is the constructive method; the separate atlas question is only whether one can compress that exact-input elimination output into compact human-readable regions without claiming a standalone parameter-uniform atlas theorem here.
statements.justification: The unconditional optimizer contribution is a special-purpose exact semialgebraic solver and certificate for this quartic law program, rather than a broad field-level randomized-saturation design theorem.
statements.gap: The contribution here is the terminating exact-input construction specialized to this quartic saturation program; the note does not separately prove or package a parameter-uniform branch atlas over Theta.
statements.consumer: Given exact symbolic or algebraic input data for (pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4), this theorem terminates, returns a globally optimal saturation law, and certifies it.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Let theta=(pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4), and treat its coordinates as exact elements of the real-closed-field presentation supplied with the input. If pbar=0 and nu in N(pbar), then integral u dnu(u)=0 while u>=0, so nu is delta_0. If pbar=1, then integral (1-u) dnu(u)=0 while 1-u>=0, so nu is delta_1. Thus the boundary algorithm that outputs delta_pbar is terminating, feasible, and globally optimal, and the certificate is the direct singleton certificate. Assume now that 0<pbar<1. Put p=pbar, q=1-p, C=pq, A=q-p, and D=[-p,q]. By lem:centered-pushforward-program, minimizing V over N(pbar) is equivalent to minimizing over probability laws mu on D with integral d dmu(d)=0, after the change of variables d=u-p. By prop:support-at-most-three, at least one global minimizer has support size at most three. By lem:profile-value-reduction, on a fixed second-moment slice s the objective is V_0+integral f(d)dmu(d)-V_4s^2, where f(d)=V_1d^2+V_3d^3+V_4d^4. By lem:explicit-profile-value, the slice infimum is explicit: F(0)=0 and, for 0<=s<=C, F(s) is the smaller of the two displayed quadratic interval minima Phi_2 over I_2(s) and Phi_3 over I_3(s), and H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4s^2. The Phi_2 branch is attained by the two-root law x^2-tx-s=0; when optimized over all two-point laws it is equivalently represented by the singleton S1 and the two endpoint cubic branches S2, by lem:two-point-endpoint-reduction. The Phi_3 branch is attained by the endpoint-interior law on {-p,r,q} with the weights stated in lem:explicit-profile-value; this is the three-point family S3, with its boundary subfamilies obtained by allowing active inequalities in 0<=s<=C and -s/q<=r<=s/p. Therefore the global value of the law program is the minimum over the finite union of the branch programs S1, S2, and S3 described in the statement. Each branch has a compact semialgebraic feasible set after multiplying inequalities by the positive constants p and q, and each branch objective is polynomial in the branch variables and the exact input parameters. The finitely many endpoint, active-boundary, and stationary equations listed in the statement are precisely the KKT equations for these one-dimensional or two-dimensional polynomial branch objectives on their compact semialgebraic domains. By lem:semialgebraic-optimization-algorithms, finite polynomial optimization over these compact semialgebraic sets admits a terminating exact procedure using Tarski-Seidenberg elimination, CAD, Thom encodings, root isolation, and Sturm sign tests; the procedure returns feasible algebraic candidates, discards infeasible roots by exact sign evaluation, orders their objective values exactly, and selects a minimum. Construct nu_alg from the selected branch: S1 gives delta_pbar; S2 uses the endpoint two-point formulas; S3 uses the endpoint-interior weights from lem:explicit-profile-value and then pushes forward by d -> p+d. The constructed law lies in N(pbar), and its exact algebraic V-value equals the selected branch value. Since the branch union contains a support-at-most-three global minimizer and the branch values equal the profile values supplied by lem:explicit-profile-value, no admissible law has V-value below V(nu_alg). Hence nu_alg minimizes V over N(pbar). Finally, the stated certificate is finite and exact. Direct boundary and degenerate-branch cases are certified by singleton feasibility or exact candidate comparison. On an interior contact pattern, the contact and tangency equations determine multipliers a,b,c for Q_{a,b,c}; nonnegativity of the quartic residual on D is decidable by Sturm/root-isolation because Q is a univariate quartic, and the outer inequality H(s)>=V(nu_alg) on [0,C] is decidable by the same semialgebraic machinery using the explicit piecewise-algebraic formula for H from lem:explicit-profile-value. When these checks hold, thm:global-certificate gives an independent global-optimality certificate. Thus for exact symbolic or algebraic input the algorithm is complete, exact, terminating, returns a globally optimal saturation law, and certifies it; the separate task of compressing the exact CAD/Thom output into a short printed atlas is not part of this theorem.

statements.statement: The exact optimizer over the singleton/two-point saturation class is closed form. Let p=pbar, q=1-p, A=q-p, and q_theta(t)=V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2. The value above V_0 is the minimum of 0, the left endpoint cubic branch g_L(t)=p(p+t)q_theta(t) on t in [-p,A], and the right endpoint cubic branch g_R(t)=q(q-t)q_theta(t) on t in [A,q]. The complete candidate set is t=-p,A,q together with the roots in [-p,A] of V_1+pV_3+2(V_3+pV_4)t+3V_4t^2=0 and the roots in [A,q] of -V_1+qV_3+2(qV_4-V_3)t-3V_4t^2=0. Choosing the candidate with smallest value gives the two-point-class optimum: value 0 gives delta_pbar; a left-branch minimizer gives centered support {-p,p+t} with weights (p+t)/(2p+t) at -p and p/(2p+t) at p+t; and a right-branch minimizer gives centered support {t-q,q} with weights q/(2q-t) at t-q and (q-t)/(2q-t) at q. The two-versus-three-point comparison is the exact algebraic comparison performed inside thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorithm; it is not a missing piece of this complete two-point result.
statements.justification: The practically relevant skewed design should have an inspectable formula rather than only a black-box algebraic description.
statements.gap: Prior randomized-saturation design results optimize over restricted menus but do not give this endpoint-skewed two-atom KKT solution inside the unrestricted law program.
statements.consumer: A practitioner can compute the exact singleton/two-point optimum by checking two endpoint cubic branches and at most two quadratic stationary equations, then use the constructive algorithm for the separate global comparison.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Assume 0<pbar<1 and write p=pbar, q=1-p, A=q-p, D=[-p,q], and q_theta(t)=V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2. In centered coordinates, the only one-point mean-zero law is delta_0, hence its value above V_0 is 0. A nondegenerate two-point mean-zero law has support {a,-b} with 0<a<=q and 0<b<=p, mass b/(a+b) at a, and mass a/(a+b) at -b. By lem:two-point-centered-objective, with t=a-b its value above V_0 is ab q_theta(t). By lem:two-point-endpoint-reduction, the minimum over all singleton and two-point centered laws is therefore the minimum of the singleton value 0, the left endpoint branch obtained by setting b=p, and the right endpoint branch obtained by setting a=q. The left endpoint branch has a=p+t and t in [-p,A], so its value is g_L(t)=p(p+t)q_theta(t). The right endpoint branch has b=q-t and t in [A,q], so its value is g_R(t)=q(q-t)q_theta(t). These two compact intervals include all endpoint and degenerate cases, with t=A representing the common two-endpoint law. Since g_L and g_R are cubic polynomials on compact intervals, their minima occur at endpoints or at roots of their derivatives. Direct differentiation gives g_L'(t)=p[V_1+pV_3+2(V_3+pV_4)t+3V_4t^2] and g_R'(t)=q[-V_1+qV_3+2(qV_4-V_3)t-3V_4t^2]. Because p and q are positive, the stationary equations are exactly the two equations displayed in the statement. Thus the complete candidate set is t=-p,A,q together with the roots of the left derivative equation that lie in [-p,A] and the roots of the right derivative equation that lie in [A,q]. Choosing the candidate with the smallest exact value gives the two-point-class optimum. If the selected value is 0, the law delta_pbar attains it. If the selected candidate lies on the left branch, its centered support is {-p,p+t}; the mean-zero weights are (p+t)/(2p+t) at -p and p/(2p+t) at p+t. If the selected candidate lies on the right branch, its centered support is {t-q,q}; the mean-zero weights are q/(2q-t) at t-q and (q-t)/(2q-t) at q. These formulas are obtained by solving the single equation that the weighted centered mean is zero, and the denominators are positive on the stated intervals except for degenerate endpoints, where the zero-weight atom is removed. The comparison between this closed-form singleton/two-point optimum and possible three-point endpoint-interior optima is not part of the two-point optimization itself; it is exactly the algebraic comparison carried out inside thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorithm.

statements.statement: If C is a compact convex subset of R^n and an open convex set O is disjoint from C, then there is a nonzero affine functional that weakly separates C from O; in particular, a finite-dimensional compact convex moment set has a supporting hyperplane at any exposed lower boundary point.
statements.proof_tex: Source/proof. This is the finite-dimensional strict separation/supporting-hyperplane theorem for convex sets; see Rockafellar, Convex Analysis, Princeton University Press, 1970, Theorem 11.3 and Corollary 11.6.1. Applied to a compact convex set and a disjoint open convex ray, it gives the separating affine functional used in the bounded moment-slice duality lemma.

statements.statement: If I is a compact interval of R, then P(I) is weakly compact.
statements.proof_tex: Source/proof. This is the compact-space case of Prokhorov's theorem: on a compact metric space every family of probability measures is tight, so the whole space P(I) is relatively compact for weak convergence, and it is weakly closed. Equivalently, one may combine the Riesz representation theorem with Banach-Alaoglu. Either route yields weak compactness of P(I).

statements.statement: If I is a compact interval of R and g:I -> R is bounded and continuous, then mu |-> int g dmu is weakly continuous on P(I).
statements.proof_tex: Source/proof. This is the bounded-continuous-test-function characterization of weak convergence, equivalently the Portmanteau theorem: mu_n converges weakly to mu if and only if int g dmu_n -> int g dmu for every bounded continuous function g.

statements.statement: If K is compact and h:K -> R is continuous, then h attains its minimum on K.
statements.proof_tex: Source/proof. This is the Weierstrass extreme-value theorem.

statements.statement: Let D_pbar be the compact centered-support interval and let f:D_pbar -> R be continuous. Then the moment image C_f={(int 1 dmu, int d dmu, int d^2 dmu, int f(d) dmu): mu in P(D_pbar)} is a compact convex subset of R^4.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Because D_pbar is a compact interval, lem:compact-support-probability-laws-weakly-compact gives weak compactness of P(D_pbar). The four coordinate maps mu |-> int 1 dmu, mu |-> int d dmu, mu |-> int d^2 dmu, and mu |-> int f(d) dmu are weakly continuous by lem:bounded-continuous-integrals-weakly-continuous, since 1, d, d^2, and f are bounded continuous functions on D_pbar. Therefore the joint moment map from P(D_pbar) into R^4 is continuous, so its image C_f is compact. The same map is affine in mu, so the image of any convex combination of probability laws is the corresponding convex combination of image points. Hence C_f is convex.

statements.statement: Fix s for which N_s(pbar,s) is nonempty and set f(d)=V_1d^2+V_3d^3+V_4d^4. A law nu_star in N_s(pbar,s) minimizes int f(d) d(T_pbar)_#nu(d) over N_s(pbar,s) if and only if there exist a,b,c in R such that Q_{a,b,c}(d)=f(d)-a-bd-cd^2 is nonnegative on D_pbar and vanishes on T_pbar(supp(nu_star)).
statements.proof_tex:
Proof. Put mu_star=(T_pbar)_#nu_star. The sufficiency direction is immediate. If Q_{a,b,c}>=0 on D_pbar, then for any centered law mu with int d dmu=0 and int d^2 dmu=s,
 int f dmu = a + b int d dmu + c int d^2 dmu + int Q dmu >= a + c s.
If Q vanishes on the support of mu_star, equality holds for mu_star, so mu_star minimizes the slice objective.
For necessity, pass to centered laws on the compact interval D_pbar and assume mu_star is a slice minimizer. Let

alpha = inf{int f dmu : mu is a probability law on D_pbar, int d dmu=0, int d^2 dmu=s}.

Then alpha=int f dmu_star. Let C={(int 1 dmu, int d dmu, int d^2 dmu, int f dmu): mu is a probability law on D_pbar}. By lem:moment-image-compact-convex, this moment image C is compact and convex. The point (1,0,s,alpha) lies in C because mu_star realizes the slice infimum, and it lies on the lower boundary of C because every point of C with first three coordinates (1,0,s) has fourth coordinate at least alpha. Let O={(1,0,s,y): y<alpha}. Then O is an open convex ray disjoint from C. By lem:finite-dimensional-supporting-hyperplane there is a supporting hyperplane separating C from O. Evaluating the separating functional on the vertical ray O and at (1,0,s,alpha) shows that its coefficient on the fourth coordinate is positive. After normalizing that coefficient to one and using that the first coordinate is always int 1 dmu=1, the hyperplane gives constants a,b,c such that Q_{a,b,c}(d)=f(d)-a-bd-cd^2>=0 for every d in D_pbar. Integrating Q_{a,b,c} against mu_star gives zero, because mu_star has mean zero, second moment s, and objective value alpha. A nonnegative continuous function with zero integral under mu_star must vanish on the topological support of mu_star. Translating the support back by T_pbar gives the stated support condition.

statements.statement: In the compact interval D_pbar, every nonempty moment slice of probability laws satisfying int 1 dmu=1, int d dmu=0, and int d^2 dmu=s has an extreme point minimizer for each continuous linear objective, and every such extreme point is supported on at most three points.
statements.proof_tex: Source/proof. This is the standard extreme-point theorem for moment sets. See Winkler, Extreme points of moment sets, Mathematics of Operations Research 13(4), 1988, Theorem 2.1, which characterizes extreme points of sets of probability measures with finitely many generalized moment constraints; with the three functions 1,d,d^2, an extreme probability measure has at most three atoms. The existence of an extreme minimizer of a continuous affine functional on a nonempty compact convex moment set is the Bauer maximum principle applied to the negative of the functional, or equivalently the Krein-Milman theorem followed by affinity of the objective. These cited results supply exactly the compact interval, three-moment version used here.

statements.statement: For 0<pbar<1, the map T_pbar(u)=u-pbar sends each nu in N(pbar) to a probability law mu on D_pbar with int d dmu(d)=0, and m_r(nu)=int d^r dmu(d) for r=2,3,4. Conversely, each probability law mu on D_pbar with int d dmu(d)=0 pushes forward under d -> pbar+d to a law in N(pbar). For every nu in N(pbar), 0<=m_2(nu)<=pbar(1-pbar).
statements.proof_tex: Let mu=(T_pbar)_#nu. Since nu is supported on [0,1], T_pbar sends [0,1] into D_pbar=[-pbar,1-pbar], so mu is supported on D_pbar. By the change-of-variables formula, int d dmu(d)=int (u-pbar)dnu(u)=int u dnu(u)-pbar=0 because nu belongs to N(pbar). The same change of variables gives int d^r dmu(d)=int (u-pbar)^r dnu(u)=m_r(nu) for r=2,3,4. Conversely, if mu is supported on D_pbar and has mean zero, then the pushforward nu under d -> pbar+d is supported on [0,1] and int u dnu(u)=int (pbar+d)dmu(d)=pbar, so nu is in N(pbar). Finally, for U in [0,1] with law nu and E U=pbar, m_2(nu)=E[(U-pbar)^2]=E[U^2]-pbar^2<=E[U]-pbar^2=pbar(1-pbar), while nonnegativity is immediate.

statements.statement: Assume 0 < pbar < 1. Let f(d)=V_1 d^2+V_3 d^3+V_4 d^4 and H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4 s^2. For every nonempty slice N_s(pbar,s), H(s)=inf{V(nu):nu in N_s(pbar,s)}. Moreover, for every nu in N_s(pbar,s), V(nu)=V_0+int f(d)d(T_pbar)_#nu(d)-V_4 s^2.
statements.proof_tex: Assume 0<pbar<1 and fix nu in N_s(pbar,s). Set mu=(T_pbar)_#nu. By lem:centered-pushforward-program and def:centered-moments, m_2(nu)=s and m_r(nu)=int d^r dmu(d) for r=2,3,4. Substituting into def:variance-functional yields V(nu)=V_0+int [V_1d^2+V_3d^3+V_4d^4]dmu(d)-V_4 s^2. The term V_0-V_4s^2 is constant on the slice. Taking the infimum over nu in N_s(pbar,s) and using def:profile-value gives inf_{nu in N_s(pbar,s)}V(nu)=V_0+F(s)-V_4s^2. By def:outer-certificate, the right side is H(s).

statements.statement: For 0<pbar<1, the objective V attains a minimum over N(pbar). For every s in [0,pbar(1-pbar)] with N_s(pbar,s) nonempty, the profile value F(s) is attained on N_s(pbar,s).
statements.proof_tex: Proof. The ambient law space P([0,1]) is weakly compact by lem:compact-support-probability-laws-weakly-compact. The maps nu |-> int u dnu(u) and nu |-> int (u-pbar)^r dnu(u) for r=2,3,4 are weakly continuous by lem:bounded-continuous-integrals-weakly-continuous, because their integrands are bounded and continuous on [0,1]. Hence N(pbar), cut out by the closed mean constraint int u dnu=pbar, is weakly compact. Likewise N_s(pbar,s), obtained by adding the closed second-moment constraint, is weakly compact whenever nonempty. The formula in def:variance-functional is a continuous function of the moments m_2,m_3,m_4, so V is continuous on N(pbar). By lem:continuous-function-attains-minimum-on-compact-set, V attains its minimum there. The profile objective in def:profile-value is also the integral of a bounded continuous polynomial in u-pbar, hence continuous on each compact nonempty slice N_s(pbar,s), so lem:continuous-function-attains-minimum-on-compact-set gives attainment of F(s) on that slice.

statements.statement: Let mu be a probability law on D_pbar with int d dmu(d)=0, let s=int d^2 dmu(d)>0, m_3=int d^3 dmu(d), and m_4=int d^4 dmu(d). Then t=m_3/s lies in D_pbar and m_4-s^2>=m_3^2/s.
statements.proof_tex: Because s>0, the measure rho defined by drho(d)=d^2 dmu(d)/s is a probability law. Its support is contained in D_pbar, so its mean t=int d drho(d)=m_3/s lies in the convex hull of D_pbar, which is D_pbar itself. The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality applied to the square-integrable random variables d^2 and 1 under mu gives (int d^2 dmu)^2 <= int d^4 dmu. A sharper form applied to d^2-s and d gives (int (d^2-s)d dmu)^2 <= int (d^2-s)^2 dmu * int d^2 dmu. Since int d dmu=0, the left side is m_3^2 and the first factor on the right is m_4-s^2. Thus m_3^2 <= (m_4-s^2)s, which is the claimed inequality.

statements.statement: For 0<pbar<1 and every s in [0,pbar(1-pbar)], the slice N_s(pbar,s) is nonempty.
statements.proof_tex:
Let D_pbar=[-pbar,1-pbar]. The centered two-endpoint law

mu_max = (1-pbar) delta_{-pbar} + pbar delta_{1-pbar}

has mean zero because (1-pbar)(-pbar)+pbar(1-pbar)=0, and second moment pbar(1-pbar). For s in [0,pbar(1-pbar)], put lambda=s/[pbar(1-pbar)] and define

mu_s = (1-lambda) delta_0 + lambda mu_max.

Then mu_s is supported on D_pbar, has mean zero, and has second moment s. Push mu_s forward by d -> pbar+d to obtain nu_s on [0,1]. By lem:centered-pushforward-program, nu_s lies in N(pbar), and by construction m_2(nu_s)=s. Hence nu_s is in N_s(pbar,s).

statements.statement: Let 0<a<=1-pbar and 0<b<=pbar. Let mu place mass b/(a+b) at a and mass a/(a+b) at -b, and let nu be its pushforward under d -> pbar+d. Then nu in N(pbar), m_2(nu)=ab, m_3(nu)=ab(a-b), m_4(nu)-m_2(nu)^2=ab(a-b)^2, and V(nu)-V_0=ab[V_1+V_3(a-b)+V_4(a-b)^2].
statements.proof_tex:
The centered mean is

[b/(a+b)]a + [a/(a+b)](-b)=0,

so the pushforward law nu has mean pbar and hence belongs to N(pbar). Direct calculation gives

m_2 = [b/(a+b)]a^2 + [a/(a+b)]b^2 = ab,

m_3 = [b/(a+b)]a^3 - [a/(a+b)]b^3 = ab(a-b),

and

m_4 = [b/(a+b)]a^4 + [a/(a+b)]b^4 = ab(a^2-ab+b^2).

Therefore

m_4-m_2^2 = ab(a^2-ab+b^2)-a^2b^2 = ab(a-b)^2.

Substituting these three identities into def:variance-functional yields

V(nu)-V_0 = V_1 ab + V_3 ab(a-b) + V_4 ab(a-b)^2 = ab[V_1+V_3(a-b)+V_4(a-b)^2].

statements.statement: Cai-Pouget-Abadie-Airoldi randomized-saturation variance expansion. For an equal-cluster randomized-saturation design under the linear interference model and the regularity regime M -> infinity and inf_j N_j -> infinity used in Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi (2022), if pi is the cluster treatment-proportion vector with mean pbar, mu_2(pi) is its centered second moment, and mu_3(pi), mu_4(pi) are its centered third and fourth moments, then Var_Z[hat_tau] = V_0 + V_1 mu_2(pi) + V_2 mu_2(pi)^2 + V_3 mu_3(pi) + V_4 (mu_4(pi) - mu_2(pi)^2) + o(N^-1). Under the block-fixed isolated-cluster specialization, the coefficient V_2 is zero.
statements.proof_tex: Source proof. Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi, "Optimizing Randomized and Deterministic Saturation Designs under Interference," arXiv:2203.09682, Theorem 9, Eq. (4), states the displayed expansion of the design variance of the difference-in-means estimator in terms of the centered second, third, and fourth moments of the treatment-proportion vector, with an o(N^-1) remainder in the regime M -> infinity and inf_j N_j -> infinity. Their Section 2.4.2 and Corollary 12 give the simplified coefficient formulas under the block-fixed specialization, and the discussion immediately following Corollary 12 states that in the extreme case of isolated clusters the V_2 coefficient is zero. This cited theorem and corollary are the published source for the expansion used here.

statements.statement: Let pi^(M) be saturation vectors in [0,1]^M whose empirical laws L_M(pi^(M)) converge weakly to nu in P([0,1]), and let pbar be fixed. Then for r in {2,3,4}, M^(-1) sum_{j=1}^M (pi_j^(M)-pbar)^r -> integral_[0,1] (u-pbar)^r d nu(u). In particular, if nu in N(pbar), the limit is m_r(nu).
statements.proof_tex: Proof. For each r in {2,3,4}, the function phi_r(u) = (u-pbar)^r is continuous and bounded on the compact interval [0,1]. Weak convergence of L_M(pi^(M)) to nu therefore implies integral phi_r dL_M(pi^(M)) -> integral phi_r dnu. By the definition of the empirical law, integral phi_r dL_M(pi^(M)) = M^(-1) sum_{j=1}^M (pi_j^(M)-pbar)^r. If nu in N(pbar), def:centered-moments identifies the limiting integral with m_r(nu).

statements.statement: For every nu in N(pbar), 0 <= m_2(nu) <= pbar(1-pbar). The lower bound is attained by delta_pbar, with equality only when nu=delta_pbar. The upper bound is attained by (1-pbar)delta_0+pbar delta_1; when 0<pbar<1, equality in the upper bound forces nu=(1-pbar)delta_0+pbar delta_1.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Let U denote the identity random variable on [0,1] under the law nu. By def:admissible-law-class, E_nu[U]=pbar, and by def:centered-moments, m_2(nu)=E_nu[(U-pbar)^2]. The lower bound m_2(nu)>=0 follows from nonnegativity of the square. Equality holds if and only if U=pbar nu-almost surely, which is exactly nu=delta_pbar; conversely delta_pbar is admissible and has m_2(delta_pbar)=0. For the upper bound, expand m_2(nu)=E_nu[U^2]-2pbar E_nu[U]+pbar^2=E_nu[U^2]-pbar^2. Since 0<=U<=1, U^2<=U pointwise, so E_nu[U^2]<=E_nu[U]=pbar. Hence m_2(nu)<=pbar-pbar^2=pbar(1-pbar). For nu_B=(1-pbar)delta_0+pbar delta_1, E_{nu_B}[U]=pbar and E_{nu_B}[U^2]=pbar, so m_2(nu_B)=pbar(1-pbar), proving attainment. If 0<pbar<1 and equality holds in the upper bound, then equality must hold in U^2<=U nu-almost surely, so U belongs to {0,1} nu-almost surely. The mean constraint then forces nu({1})=pbar and nu({0})=1-pbar, hence nu=nu_B.

statements.statement: Finite polynomial optimization over compact semialgebraic subsets of a real closed field generated by exact input parameters admits a terminating exact procedure that returns algebraic sample minimizers, exact ordered objective values, and certificates of univariate polynomial nonnegativity on intervals via Thom encodings, cylindrical algebraic decomposition, quantifier elimination, real-root isolation, and Sturm sign tests.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. This is the standard algorithmic real algebraic geometry substrate: Tarski's decision method for real closed fields (Tarski, 1951), Seidenberg's quantifier-elimination theorem for real closed fields (Seidenberg, 1954), Collins's cylindrical algebraic decomposition with sample points and Thom encodings (Collins, 1975), and the classical Sturm theorem for counting and ordering real roots of univariate polynomials. These results imply decidability of finite systems of polynomial equalities and inequalities, construction of algebraic sample points in each cell, exact comparison of algebraic objective values, and verification of polynomial nonnegativity on compact intervals.

statements.statement: For 0<pbar<1, with p=pbar, q=1-p, A=q-p, and q_theta(t)=V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2, the minimum of V-V_0 over mean-zero laws supported on at most two centered points in [-p,q] equals the minimum over the singleton value 0 and the two endpoint branches g_L(t)=p(p+t)q_theta(t) for t in [-p,A] and g_R(t)=q(q-t)q_theta(t) for t in [A,q].
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Any nondegenerate two-point mean-zero centered law has support {-b,a} with 0<a<=q and 0<b<=p and unique weights b/(a+b) at a and a/(a+b) at -b. By lem:two-point-centered-objective, V-V_0=ab q_theta(a-b). Put t=a-b. For fixed t, feasibility is b in [max(0,-t), min(p,q-t)] and a=b+t. The product ab=b(b+t) is increasing over this feasible interval, so its maximum is obtained at b=p when t<=A and at a=q when t>=A. If q_theta(t)<0, the smallest value for that t uses this maximum product; if q_theta(t)>=0, the degenerate singleton value 0 is no worse. Therefore no two-point law improves on the union of the singleton and the two endpoint branches, and every point on those branches is itself a feasible two-point or limiting singleton law.

statements.statement: For a compact interval D_pbar and any s for which the centered slice {mu in P(D_pbar): integral d dmu=0, integral d^2 dmu=s} is nonempty, that slice is compact and convex in the weak topology, and every continuous affine functional on it attains its minimum.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Because D_pbar is a compact interval, lem:compact-support-probability-laws-weakly-compact gives weak compactness of P(D_pbar). The constraint maps mu |-> integral d dmu and mu |-> integral d^2 dmu are weakly continuous by lem:bounded-continuous-integrals-weakly-continuous, since d and d^2 are bounded continuous on D_pbar. Hence the centered slice is a weakly closed subset of the compact set P(D_pbar), so it is weakly compact. It is convex because the two moment constraints are affine in mu. Any continuous affine functional on the slice is continuous on a compact set, so lem:continuous-function-attains-minimum-on-compact-set gives an attained minimum.

statements.statement: Let 0<pbar<1, p=pbar, q=1-p, C=pq, A=q-p, and 0<s<C. If mu is a probability law on [-p,q] with E X=0, E X^2=s, m3=E X^3, and m4=E X^4, then t=m3/s lies in I_2(s)=[s/p-p,q-s/q], r=(m3-A s)/(s-C) lies in I_3(s)=[-s/q,s/p], m4>=s^2+s t^2 with equality on the two-root law x^2-tx-s=0, and m4<=(1-3C)s+(s-C)(r^2+A r) with equality on the endpoint-interior law on {-p,r,q} with the stated weights.
statements.proof_tex: Proof. Let X have law mu on D=[-p,q]. First bound m3. Since (q-X)(X+s/q)^2>=0 on D, expansion and E X=0 give 0<=E[(q-X)(X+s/q)^2]=q s-m3-s^2/q, hence t=m3/s<=q-s/q. Since (X+p)(X-s/p)^2>=0 on D, 0<=E[(X+p)(X-s/p)^2]=m3+p s-s^2/p, hence t>=s/p-p. Thus t lies in I_2(s). Because m3=s t=A s+(s-C)r, the affine relation t=A+(s-C)r/s maps r=-s/q to q-s/q and r=s/p to s/p-p; since s-C<0, this is equivalent to r in I_3(s). For the lower envelope, expand (X^2-tX-s)^2. Using E X=0 and m3=s t gives E[(X^2-tX-s)^2]=m4-s^2-s t^2, so m4>=s^2+s t^2. Equality holds iff X^2-tX-s=0 mu-a.s.; the endpoint inequalities above are exactly the condition that the two roots lie in [-p,q]. For the upper envelope, (X+p)(q-X)(X-r)^2 is nonnegative on [-p,q]. Since (X+p)(q-X)=C+A X-X^2 and m3=A s+(s-C)r, expansion gives E[(X+p)(q-X)(X-r)^2]=(1-3C)s+(s-C)(r^2+A r)-m4. Hence m4 is bounded above by the displayed expression. Equality holds iff X is supported on {-p,r,q}, with deletion of zero-weight or coincident endpoint cases. Solving w_-+w_r+w_+=1, -p w_-+r w_r+q w_+=0, and p^2w_-+r^2w_r+q^2w_+=s gives w_-=(s+rq)/(p+r), w_r=(C-s)/((p+r)(q-r)), and w_+=(s-pr)/(q-r). The inequalities r in [-s/q,s/p] make these weights nonnegative, with endpoint limiting interpretations.

statements.statement: Let nu = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} be in N(pbar), with K <= 3, M,m positive integers, N=Mm, and N pbar in Z. The grid-split rounding handle can be run to produce pi_rd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) and a coupling gamma_rd of nu and L_M(pi_rd) such that integral |u-v| d gamma_rd(u,v) <= 2K/M + 2/m <= 6/M + 2/m.
statements.proof_tex: Choose integers n_1,...,n_K with sum_ell n_ell = M and |n_ell/M - w_ell| <= 1/M for every ell, for example by the usual largest-remainder rounding of the masses. Let nu_M^0 = sum_ell (n_ell/M) delta_{u_ell}. Since nu and nu_M^0 are supported on the same K points, couple the common mass at each point to itself and move only the excess mass. The total excess mass is at most sum_ell |n_ell/M - w_ell| <= K/M, and all moves occur inside [0,1], so the transport cost from nu to nu_M^0 is at most K/M. Now form an M-vector x by listing n_ell copies of u_ell for each ell. Its empirical law is nu_M^0. Replace each x_j by y_j = floor(m x_j)/m, with the convention y_j=1 when x_j=1. Then y_j belongs to {0,1/m,...,1}, |y_j-x_j| <= 1/m, and the paired empirical laws have average displacement at most 1/m. Let S = N pbar, an integer by hypothesis, and let T = sum_{j=1}^M m y_j. If S > T, increase grid coordinates one unit at a time, never above m; if S < T, decrease grid coordinates one unit at a time, never below 0. This is feasible because 0 <= S <= M m, so the total upward capacity is M m - T >= S - T when S >= T, and the total downward capacity is T >= T - S when S <= T. Let z_j be the resulting grid coordinates divided by m, and set pi_rd=(z_1,...,z_M). Then pi_rd is on the 1/m grid and M^{-1} sum_j pi_rd,j = S/(M m)=pbar, so pi_rd lies in Pi_{M,m}(pbar). This is exactly the mass-round, grid-split, and final-balancing procedure described in def:grid-split-rounding. The cost of the final balancing coupling from the empirical law of y to the empirical law of z is M^{-1} sum_j |z_j-y_j| = |S-T|/(M m). Since nu has mean pbar and |M^{-1} sum_j x_j - pbar| <= K/M while |M^{-1} sum_j y_j - M^{-1} sum_j x_j| <= 1/m, we have |S-T|/(M m) = |pbar - M^{-1} sum_j y_j| <= K/M + 1/m. Concatenating the three couplings gives a coupling of nu and L_M(pi_rd) with cost at most K/M + 1/m + K/M + 1/m = 2K/M + 2/m. Since K <= 3, this is at most 6/M + 2/m.

statements.statement: Let eta and nu be probability laws on [0,1], and let gamma be any coupling of eta and nu with integral |u-v| d gamma(u,v) <= Delta. Then, for the functional V built from centered moments around pbar in [0,1], |V(eta)-V(nu)| <= (2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|) Delta.
statements.proof_tex: For r=2,3,4 set g_r(u)=(u-pbar)^r. Because u and pbar lie in [0,1], |u-pbar| <= 1, and the derivative bound |g_r'(u)| = r |u-pbar|^{r-1} <= r gives |g_r(u)-g_r(v)| <= r |u-v| for all u,v in [0,1]. Integrating this inequality under gamma yields |m_r(eta)-m_r(nu)| <= r Delta for r=2,3,4. Also 0 <= m_2(eta),m_2(nu) <= 1, so |m_2(eta)^2 - m_2(nu)^2| <= |m_2(eta)-m_2(nu)| |m_2(eta)+m_2(nu)| <= 2 |m_2(eta)-m_2(nu)| <= 4 Delta. Using def:variance-functional and cancelling V_0, we get |V(eta)-V(nu)| <= |V_1| |m_2(eta)-m_2(nu)| + |V_3| |m_3(eta)-m_3(nu)| + |V_4| (|m_4(eta)-m_4(nu)| + |m_2(eta)^2-m_2(nu)^2|) <= (2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|) Delta.

statements.statement: If pbar in {0,1}, then N(pbar)={delta_pbar}.
statements.proof_tex: Let nu be a probability law on [0,1] with integral u dnu(u)=pbar. If pbar=0, then u>=0 on [0,1] and a nonnegative random variable with mean zero is zero almost surely; hence nu=delta_0. If pbar=1, then 1-u>=0 and integral (1-u)dnu(u)=1-integral u dnu(u)=0, so 1-u=0 almost surely and nu=delta_1. The reverse inclusions delta_0 in N(0) and delta_1 in N(1) are immediate from the same mean constraint in def:admissible-law-class.

sampling_model.design: Finite-population two-stage randomized saturation design under isolated partial interference; the first-stage design law nu chooses cluster saturations and the second stage randomizes binary treatment within clusters at the realized saturation.
sampling_model.units: N = M m fixed units partitioned into M equal-size clusters C_1, ..., C_M.
sampling_model.treatment: Binary treatment assigned within cluster j according to its realized saturation u or finite-grid counterpart pi_j.
sampling_model.overlap: Design positivity is generated by the support of nu or pi over admissible saturation levels with mean pbar.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: None. The proposal is design-based on a fixed finite population with a specialized homogeneous linear-in-share working variance expansion.
